title: Fix overlay volumes on Windows
body:
The Windows source folder path was not converted in the corresponding machine folder path when the volume was of type overlay as it does for other bind mount volumes.

Fix #25988

Overlay volumes provide faster I/O access to the local folder (but changes are ephemeral). For example, building podman from sources took 1m30s vs 2m48s with a normal volume.

```pwsh
> .\bin\windows\podman.exe build -t podman-build -f .\.devcontainer\Dockerfile .
> Measure-Command {Start-Process "C:\Users\mario\git\podman\bin\windows\podman.exe" -WorkingDirectory "C:\Users\mario\git\podman\" -ArgumentList "run -ti --rm -v C:\Users\mario\git\podman\:/podman:O -w /podman podman-build make podman" -NoNewWindow -Wait}
``` 

```release-note
Mounting local folders in a container with the overlay option on Windows used to fail. The bug has been fixed, and it's now possible to mount overlay volumes on Windows too.
```

comments.body:
> Overlay volumes provide faster I/O access to the local folder (but changes are ephemeral). For example, building podman from sources took 1m30s vs 2m48s with a normal volume.

I don't think that is the right conclusion. The local access speed should be the same, the difference is that there is a now a local tmpfs mount as writeable layer mounted so all the writes would go into tmpfs not back to the host.
At least I would guess if you would do a read only test you should see no difference.

> > Overlay volumes provide faster I/O access to the local folder (but changes are ephemeral). For example, building podman from sources took 1m30s vs 2m48s with a normal volume.
> 
> I don't think that is the right conclusion. The local access speed should be the same, the difference is that there is a now a local tmpfs mount as writeable layer mounted so all the writes would go into tmpfs not back to the host. At least I would guess if you would do a read only test you should see no difference.

Yes, write access is faster. Of course write happens on a different filesystem but some scenarios, like podman documentation build on Windows, can benefit from this.

> The new test failed on hyperV so rerunning that seems pointless? Even if it would pass in the second try it means there is a flake in that test.

That looks like a problem introduced by this PR, indeed. For what it's worth, a few Fedora jobs were failing, and I selected "run all failing."

>  I selected "run all failing."

yeah our current flake right is way to high so I totally get that rerunning all is likely the best choice in almost all cases.
